dc_title: Dr. Michael E. DeBakey, number 01
dc_subject: Cardiologists|Baylor College of Medicine--Faculty|DeBakey, Michael E. (Michael Ellis), 1908-2008|Veterinary teachers|Veterinarians
dcterms_isPartOf: College of Veterinary Medicine Image Collection
dcterms_abstract: A professional headshot image of Dr. Michael Ellis DeBakey, M.D., renowned American heart surgeon.  Dr. DeBakey (September 7, 1908 - July 11, 2008) was a Lebanese-American cardiac surgeon, scientist, and medical educator. Dr. DeBakey developed the roller pump, which became an essential component of the heart-lung machine that made open-heart surgery possible. He was one of the first to perform coronary artery bypass surgery, and in 1953 he performed the first successful carotid endarterectomy. A pioneer in the development of an artificial heart, DeBakey was the first to use an external heart pump successfully. In 1999 TAMU established the Michael E. DeBakey Institute for Comparative Cardiovascular Science and Biomedical Devices.  The Michael E. DeBakey Institute at Texas A&M College of Veterinary Medicine & Biomedical Sciences, founded as a collaboration between Texas A&M, the Baylor College of Medicine and the UT Health Science Center at Houston for cardiovascular research, was named after DeBakey.

During this same year, Waters became an Instructor at the Illinois National Guard.  In 1941,  he became Commanding Officer of the Sixth Training Regiment. dc_title: Final Review of A&M College of Texas Corps of Cadets on Main Drill Field, June 4 1938
dc_subject: Texas Agricultural and Mechanical College of Texas. Corps of Cadets|Aggie traditions|Special Events|Military exhibition drill|Main Drill Field (College Station, Tex.)|Waters, Jerome Jackson--Photograph collections
dcterms_isPartOf: College of Veterinary Medicine Image Collection
dcterms_abstract: A view of the Main Drill Field during Final Review, the last activity of the Corps of Cadets before it is dismissed for the summer.  This exercise includes a full military review of the entire Corps of Cadets, designed to formally signify the transition of command of the Corps from one year to the next.  Main Drill also served as the university's early football field prior to construction of a permanent field in 1905, as well as the site of horse-drawn artillery and infantry exercises and student pilot training in the 1920s. The Aggie Bonfire was held on the Main Drill Field from 1909 until 1955.  In 1920, A&M's Board of Directors paid tribute to former cadets killed during World War I by planting oak trees around the field, each with a marker that provided the name, class and site and date of death for each man. t

dc_title: Residence of the President of the A&M College of Texas
dc_subject: Agricultural and Mechanical College of Texas--Buildings|Ross, Lawrence Sullivan, 1838-1898--Homes and haunts|Waters, Jerome Jackson--Photograph collections|College presidents--Dwellings|Mansions|Historic buildings
dcterms_isPartOf: College of Veterinary Medicine Image Collection
dcterms_abstract: This was the first home built for the President of the Agricultural and Mechanical College of Texas in circa 1892 for Lawrence Sullivan Ross who became president in 1891.  The house was destroyed by fire on January 26, 1963 during General James Earl Rudder's term as the 16th president of A&M. Most of the furniture on the first floor was saved and some pieces were used as furnishing in the new Georgian Colonial style home that was built in 1965 as a presidential residence of the College.

dcterms_abstract: A "horse ambulance" fitted to a Ford Model T truck owned by Dr. Samuel Benton Manning, a practicing veterinarian, who became Mayor of David City from 1940-1950. Dr. Manning graduated from the Kansas City Veterinary College in 1907.  He was the son of Oscar Green Manning, and Mary Elizabeth Parnell. He was married to Margaret, and had two children, Genieve (1909) and Keith.  The truck prominently displays his name.

dcterms_abstract: An unidentified man on horseback outside of a barn that has an R.O.T.C. sign over the door.  The cavalry troops at the Agricultural and Mechanical College of Texas used horses for drill practices up until 1943.  This tradition was revived at A&M by the Class of 1974, with the naming of the unit after the then Commandant of the Corps of Cadets, Colonel Thomas R. Parsons.  As of 2016, there are 90 cadets and 50 horses in the Parsons Mounted Unit.

dc_title: Launch Pad and Tower at Cape Canaveral Missile Test Annex
dc_subject: Rockets (Aeronautics)|Launch complexes (Astronautics)|Animal space flight|Outer space--Exploration|United States. National Aeronautics and Space Administration|Space Flight
dcterms_isPartOf: College of Veterinary Medicine Image Collection
dcterms_abstract: This nighttime image of Launch Complex 14 at the Cape Canaveral Missile Test Annex in Florida was taken on October 12, 1961. The U.S. Army Corps of Engineers initiated construction of this facility on January 1956. The total cost of construction was $4,308,000.00 and would accommodate the Atlas A, B, D, Able, Agena A and D, and the Atlas Mercury rocket systems for the Mercury, Gemini Target Vehicle and Midas programs. On 21 February, 25 April and 13 September 1961, there were three successful unmanned Mercury Atlas launches from this launch pad. And on 29 November 1961 Mercury Atlas launched chimpanzee "Enos" from this location.  This pad was deactivated in February 1967 and in 1984 was declared a national historic landmark.

dcterms_abstract: A man stands next to the mare "Ciencia," winner, of the 1939 Santa Anita Derby, with her first foal. dc_subject: Agricultural and Mechanical College of Texas. School of Veterinary Medicine--Buildings|Historic buildings|Veterinarians|Francis Hall|College buildings|Special Events
dcterms_isPartOf: College of Veterinary Medicine Image Collection
dcterms_abstract: A group portrait of unidentified men and one woman on the steps of Francis Hall, the first permanent home of the School of Veterinary Medicine.  It is located on main campus adjacent to the Sterling E. Evans Library and was built in 1918 for the School of Veterinary Medicine. It is now being used as a learning facility for construction science majors. While the occasion for the photograph has not been confirmed, it may have been a meeting of the Texas Veterinary Medical Association in 1927.

dc_title: Mare, "Old Beck," and Horse-Mule Foal "Pat"
dc_subject: Mares|Foals|Animal Husbandry|Equidae|Horses-Breeding|Mules--Breeding|Horses
dcterms_isPartOf: College of Veterinary Medicine Image Collection
dcterms_abstract: The man on the left stands in a corral with a mare called '"Beck," obtained by A. and M. College in 1921, and her 3/4 horse-mule foal, "Pat," which was born in 1922. This was perceived as an oddity and resulted in much interest, as seen by the file of writings and correspondence on the subject of breeding mules with horses.  The man on right stands with another mule that could be the mare's infertile, mule-like female yearling named "Kit."

dcterms_isPartOf: College of Veterinary Medicine Image Collection
dcterms_abstract: One of the buildings housing the College of Veterinary Medicine is shown in this photograph.  Known as the "The Veterinary Medical Administration Building," it was part of the Phase II construction project that began in June of 1967 and was completed in the spring of 1969. It is one of three main buildings that made up the Veterinary Medical Complex and were closely connected by enclosed corridors running from east to west.  In addition to administrative offices of the Dean of Veterinary Medicine, this building housed the Veterinary Medical Library, classrooms, laboratories offices for the departments of Veterinary Parasitology and Pathology, a surgical suite for the small animal clinic, and an extension of the Anatomy Wing of the Veterinary Science Building that was occupied in 1955.

dc_title: Face of "Ham" the Space Chimpanzee While in Flight
dc_subject: Chimpanzees|Primates|Animal space flight|Laboratory animals|Outer space--Exploration|United States. National Aeronautics and Space Administration|Space Flight
dcterms_isPartOf: College of Veterinary Medicine Image Collection
dcterms_abstract: A close-up photo of "Ham" the Chimpanzee shown during a 5000-mile per hour ride.  This photograph was taken by an automatic 16mm camera with a film exposure of four frames per second.   "Ham," whose name was an acronym for Holloman Aerospace Medicine, became the first chimpanzee in space aboard the Mercury Redstone rocket on January 31, 1961. Ham was born in French Cameroon, West Africa in July of 1956 or 1957 and brought to the Holloman Air Force Base in New Mexico to start his training in 1959. Ham's mission paved the way for the first human astronauts. Ham died in 1983 is memorialized by a burial stone at the International Space Hall of Fame in Alamogordo, New Mexico; while his skeleton is part of the National Museum of Health and Medicine's collection.
tamudigital_local/details: Written on photograph:  NASA photograph No. 61-MR2-32. For Immediate Release 2-10-61. This photograph was taken by an automatic 16mm camera with a film exposure of four frames per second.

dc_subject: Swine Diseases|Serum|Swine|Cholera--Vaccination|Swine--Vaccination|Rietz, John H.|Veterinary services|Veterinarians|Veterinary injections|Agricultural and Mechanical College of Texas. School of Veterinary Medicine--Faculty|Classical Swine Fever
dcterms_isPartOf: College of Veterinary Medicine Image Collection
dcterms_abstract: Dr. John H. Rietz gives an injection to a pig while a man holds it still by its hind legs.  Dr. Rietz arrived at the School of Veterinary Science in the fall of 1903.  He stayed only two years and was replaced by Dr. R.P. Marsteller in the fall of 1905.  Born in 1881 in Ohio to Jacob Rietz and Tabitha J. Gray, Dr. Rietz, M.S., D.V.M, graduated from Ohio State University.  In 1903, he was  recognized for his research in the control of hog cholera, brucellosis, parasites of chickens, and rabies in sheep.  After leaving Texas, he worked for Federal Serum Farm in Ames, Iowa (1912-1919) and later served as an instructor at Ohio State University in 1926.  He also served as a Professor in the Department of Animal Pathology of West Virginia University from 1927 to 1948.

dc_title: Dr. Michael E. DeBakey, number 02
dc_subject: Cardiologists|Baylor College of Medicine--Faculty|DeBakey, Michael E. (Michael Ellis), 1908-2008|Veterinary teachers|Veterinarians
dcterms_isPartOf: College of Veterinary Medicine Image Collection
dcterms_abstract: A professional headshot image of Dr. Michael Ellis DeBakey, M.D., renowned American heart surgeon.  Dr. DeBakey (September 7, 1908 - July 11, 2008) was a Lebanese-American cardiac surgeon, scientist, and medical educator. Dr. DeBakey developed the roller pump, which became an essential component of the heart-lung machine that made open-heart surgery possible. He was one of the first to perform coronary artery bypass surgery, and in 1953 he performed the first successful carotid endarterectomy. A pioneer in the development of an artificial heart, DeBakey was the first to use an external heart pump successfully. In 1999 TAMU established the Michael E. DeBakey Institute for Comparative Cardiovascular Science and Biomedical Devices.  The Michael E. DeBakey Institute at Texas A&M College of Veterinary Medicine & Biomedical Sciences, founded as a collaboration between Texas A&M, the Baylor College of Medicine and the UT Health Science Center at Houston for cardiovascular research, was named after DeBakey.

dcterms_abstract: A group photo of a meeting of some importance, possibly a class reunion or an annual meeting of the Texas Veterinary Association.  The group is posing outside the old wood framed veterinary hospital.   Located on the corner of Spence and Roberts Streets, this was the second building used as a veterinary hospital. It was built in 1908 and had a central 36 X 36 foot clinic room, eight box stalls, four tie stalls, an office, feed room, janitors room and hay loft. The east wing of this building later became the anti-hop cholera serum laboratory until a brick serum lab was built in about 1917.

dcterms_abstract: A newspaper clipping shows a photograph of Tillie Alcartra, the "former world championmilch [sic] cow." and its calf.  The caption appears as: "$50,000 Sacrifice to Disease."  According to the first paragraph, this cow "...has been killed in the campaign in California to stamp out the hoof and mouth disease. Thousands of cattle, including entire prizewinning herds, have been driven into trenches, shot and their bodies covered with quicklime. Indemnity on Tillie was $750, and the state and federal governments each paid her owner, Fred Hartsook, $40 upon the execution. The calf shown with her in this picture recently was sold for $61,000."  [Publication was not identified.]

dc_title: Dr. George T. Edds of Waco, Texas and  Dr. Kid McCoy of Kealakekua, Hawaii
dc_subject: Veterinary teachers|Veterinarians|Texas A & M University. College of Veterinary Medicine--Benefactors|Edds, George T.|Texas A & M University. College of Veterinary Medicine--Alumni and alumnae|McCoy, Kid|College department heads|Special Events
dcterms_isPartOf: College of Veterinary Medicine Image Collection
dcterms_abstract: Dr. George T. Edds of Waco, Texas (left), shakes hands with Dr. Kid McCoy of Kealakekua, Hawaii (right), in front of a curtain and behind a microphone at an unknown event. Dr. Edds (A&M College of Texas, Class of '36) helped establish an endowment at the College of Veterinary Medicine in 1984.  He served as the former director/vice president of Fort Dodge Laboratories (1950-62) and the chair of the Department of Veterinary  Science at University of Florida (1962-71). Meanwhile, Dr. McCoy became the first territorial veterinarian for Hawaii.  In 1963, he founded Keauhou Veterinary Hospital, the first AAHA accredited small animal practice on the island of Hawaii.

dcterms_abstract: An unidentified student holds a young research goat after a day of classes at the College of Veterinary Medicine. According to an article from 2010 in the "Student Perspectives" section of the College's website: ""Another amazing thing about veterinary school is the unique opportunities that randomly occur. ...Another day, after learning several basic cattle skills, we had a little free time and went to visit the neighboring research goats.  About 12 kids (baby goats) had recently been born, and we just had to interact with them.  We had an impromptu lecture on goat herd management and how to do a quick physical exam on a goat."

dcterms_abstract: Dr. M. E. Tatum, Associate Professor of Veterinary Anatomy, instructs a student in the Gross Anatomy Laboratory.  Dr. Michael E. Tatum graduated from Texas A&M in 1963, and received his DVM in 1964 and was married to Carol Ann Barrett on October 3rd of the same year. He joined the faculty at the Veterinary College at Texas A&M in 1965 and taught for 35 years before retiring in 2000.  Many students fondly remembered taking histology, pathology and anatomy courses from him.

dcterms_abstract: A group portrait of ten men who were present during an operation to treat Cryptorchid in a five year old horse performed at the A&M College of Texas in 1899.  Dr. Mark Francis was also present at this event.  The men stand out of doors, since the College lacked indoor clinical facilities for many learning opportunities during this period.  Most of the names of the men are unidentified, as there were no student yearbooks published in 1896-1902.

He is holding a tube attached to a bottle, injecting cholera blood into the ear of the hog, which is being immunized to cholera. According to the text written on the photo, "...the virulent cholera blood injected causes the body cells to react and protective substances making its serum valuable in protecting susceptible pigs against cholera."  The activity featured in this photograph took place in the anti-hog cholera serum laboratory that occupied the east wing of the veterinary hospital built in 1908 at the corner of Spence and Robert streets.  It was used for this purpose until 1917 when a new anti-hog serum laboratory was constructed on the corner of Roberts and Bizzell streets.

dcterms_isPartOf: College of Veterinary Medicine Image Collection
dcterms_abstract: Jack D. Austen, a graduate student in the Department of Veterinary Microbiology, draws blood from a catfish to study antibody production in the field of aquatic animal medicine.  Thomas Eurell, Research Associate, from the Department of Veterinary Microbiology, assists him while Willie Mae Charanza, Veterinary Technician I, looks on.  In 1969, the College of Veterinary Medicine at Texas A&M University began to acquire and develop the expertise necessary to encompass aquatic animal medicine. At the time, it was one of the few veterinary colleges in the United States to conduct research and offer instruction on infectious and non-infectious diseases of marine mammals and of fish and shellfish, with special emphasis on marine species.

In 1969, the College of Veterinary Medicine at Texas A&M University began to acquire and develop the expertise necessary to encompass aquatic animal medicine. At the time, it was one of the few veterinary colleges in the United States to conduct research and offer instruction on infectious and non-infectious diseases of marine mammals and of fish and shellfish, with special emphasis on marine species.

dcterms_isPartOf: College of Veterinary Medicine Image Collection
dcterms_abstract: Dr. Kenneth L. Kuttler kneels as he collects a blood sample for the study of diseases of the blood in cattle.   Dr. Kuttler was Professor of Veterinary Microbiology in the Institute of Tropical Veterinary Medicine. Born on May 29, 1924 in Ft. Worth Texas, he earned his DVM in 1945 from Colorado State College and his MS in 1955 from New York State University.  In 1965 he received his PhD from the University of East Africa. He was in private practice from 1945-46 and a Captain in the Army Veterinary Corp from 1949-53.  Dr. Kuttler was a research Veterinarian for the USDA from 1953-1955 and in 1955 he became Chairman of the University of Nevada's Department of Veterinary Science until 1963. From 1963-1965 he was a research Veterinarian at the Rockefeller Foundation and from 1965 until about 1978 he was Professor and Associate Director of the Institute of Tropical Veterinary Medicine, Texas A&M University.

dcterms_abstract: A small one-story building owned by the Texas Agricultural Experiment Station has white metal siding and a sidewalk leading to its front door.  There appears to be a fence behind the building.  The Texas Agricultural Experiment Station was established in April 1887 to conduct research into every phase of the state's crop and livestock operations. It was to publish findings for use in college courses and dissemination by the Texas Agricultural Extension Service, which like the station is part of the Texas A&M University System.  Temporary stations were started at McKinney and Wichita Falls in 1893, and several other locations also had temporary stations during the agency's first century. The first permanent regional station opened in Beeville in 1894. By 1994 there were twelve regional Texas Agricultural Research and Extension centers, located throughout Texas.

dc_title: R. Wofford Cain
dc_subject: Horses|Cain, R. Wofford|Texas A & M University--Benefactors|Agricultural and Mechanical College of Texas--Alumni and alumnae
dcterms_isPartOf: College of Veterinary Medicine Image Collection
dcterms_abstract: R. Wofford Cain (on the left) poses with a horse and another unidentified man. R. Wofford Cain received a bachelor's degree in Civil Engineering in 1913 from the Agricultural and Mechanical College of Texas,  He made his fortune in the Oil and Gas Industry and served on the Board of Regents from 1966-1971.  He also served as Chairman of the Texas A&M Foundation and was a supporter of Aggie athletics. The Cain Foundation was established in 1952 by Effie Marie and Wofford Cain to support Texas educational institutions, public charities, civic organizations and medical research programs. The Cains were owners of a racehorse, but it is unknown if this is their horse in the image.

dcterms_isPartOf: College of Veterinary Medicine Image Collection
dcterms_abstract: A third-year veterinary student, Lloyd A. Crabb (middle), is rasping the sharp edges off the horses molars ("floating teeth") at a Texas Department of Corrections Criminal Justice (TDCJ) farm while an instructor, Dr. William McMullan, looks on. Meanwhile, an inmate assists with steadying the horse.  The College's partnership with the TDCJ dates back to the 1960s when the TDCJ approached Texas A&M with a proposal of a joint venture.  The program became part of students' rotations to supplement their educational experience.

dc_title: Equine Field Service: Dr. Mike Martin and Students with a Horse Patient
dc_subject: Horses|Clinical Competence|Veterinary students|Large Animal Medicine and Surgery|Veterinary services|Livestock--Inspection|Veterinary teachers|Veterinarians|Teaching Rounds|Martin, Michael|Texas A & M University. College of Medicine--Students|Education, Veterinary|Texas A & M University. College of Medicine--Faculty
dcterms_isPartOf: College of Veterinary Medicine Image Collection
dcterms_abstract: Dr. Mike Martin (in blue coveralls and white hat) with students as they examine a client's horse in a pasture.  Dr. Martin graduated from Texas A&M University with his D.V.M. in 1968 and his M.S. in 1981. Equine Field Service provides on-farm or at-home care of equine patients in the Bryan/College Station region. The team emphasis is on proper health maintenance and preventive health care. Clinicians are capable of addressing  almost any equine health issue that can occur in the field. Field Services is fully integrated with the Large Animal Hospital and any patient requiring access to more advanced or specialized care can be transferred to other colleagues in the hospital. The clinical faculty, fourth year students, and support staff are committed to provide the highest quality care for clients' horses.

tamudigital_local/details: Written on photograph: "AWARDS OF MERIT; Presented each year by the College of Veterinary Medicine to a recipient in each of the classes having completed more than one full term of work in the professional curriculum. dcterms_abstract: A graduate student takes a sample from a diseased catfish in a laboratory while a research associate (standing behind him), and a professor (on the right) looks on.  From left to right: Jack D. Austen (graduate student in Veterinary Microbiology), James E. Zeman (research associate from the Department of Veterinary Microbiology), and Donald H. Lewis, (Ph.D., Assistant Professor of Veterinary Microbiology). In 1969, the College of Veterinary Medicine at Texas A&M University began to acquire and develop the expertise necessary to encompass aquatic animal medicine. At the time, it was one of the few veterinary colleges in the United States to conduct research and offer instruction on infectious and non-infectious diseases of marine mammals and of fish and shellfish, with special emphasis on marine species.

dc_title: Space Chimpanzee Enos's Capsule, the "First Mate"
dc_subject: Outer space--Exploration|United States. National Aeronautics and Space Administration|Space Flight|Rockets (Aeronautics)|Animal space flight
dcterms_isPartOf: College of Veterinary Medicine Image Collection
dcterms_abstract: A space capsule, "First Mate," hangs from a cable or hook in preparation for the launch of the vehicle "Atlas."   The capsule contains "Enos." the second chimpanzee to be launched into space on November 29th 1961, and the first to achieve two orbits around the earth. After being brought from the Miami Rare Bird Farm on April 3, 1960 Enos completed more than 1,250 hours of training at the University of Kentucky and the Holloman Air Force Base. During the test flight they had two malfunctions. The capsule overheated and Enos received 76 electrical shocks from a malfunctioning "avoidance conditioning" test. On November 4, 1962 Enos died of shigellosis- related dysentery. His death was unrelated to his earth orbits on Mercury Atlas #5.

dc_title: Space Chimpanzee "Ham" in His Flight Couch
dc_subject: Laboratory animals|Outer space--Exploration|United States. National Aeronautics and Space Administration|Space Flight|Chimpanzees|Primates|Animal space flight
dcterms_isPartOf: College of Veterinary Medicine Image Collection
dcterms_abstract: The chimpanzee, "Ham" is secured in his flight couch with animal restraints. "Ham," whose name was an acronym for Holloman Aerospace Medicine, became the first chimpanzee in space aboard the Mercury Redstone rocket on January 31, 1961. Ham was born in French Cameroon, West Africa, in July of 1956 or 1957 and brought to the Holloman Air Force Base in New Mexico to start his training in 1959. Ham's mission paved the way for the first human astronauts. Ham died in 1983 and is memorialized by a burial stone at the International Space Hall of Fame in Alamogordo, New Mexico while his skeleton is part of the National Museum of Health and Medicine's collection.
tamudigital_local/details: Written on photograph: "Ham"- In his flight couch. Figure 6. Complete Animal Restraint.

dc_subject: Women college teachers|Deans (Education)|Veterinary teachers|Veterinary laboratories|Veterinary pharmacology--Research|Texas A & M University. College of Medicine--Faculty|Kochevar, Deborah|Texas A & M University. Department of Veterinary Physiology and Pharmacology--Faculty|Veterinary physiology--Research
dcterms_isPartOf: College of Veterinary Medicine Image Collection
dcterms_abstract: Dr. Debbie Kochevar, an Associate Professor of Veterinary Physiology & Pharmacology, holds up a glass container of red liquid as she examines it.   Dr. Kochevar received a Doctorate of Veterinary Medicine degree from Texas A&M University and a PhD degree in cellular and molecular biology from the University of Texas Southwestern Graduate School of Biomedical Sciences. After completing an NIH National Research Service Award Fellowship, she joined the faculty at Texas A&M University where she eventually was named Associate Dean until 2006.  She left A&M to be Dean at the Cummings School of Veterinary Medicine at Tufts University.

dcterms_isPartOf: College of Veterinary Medicine Image Collection
dcterms_abstract: Dr. Bob Burghardt of Veterinary Anatomy and Public Health is shown working in the Image Analysis Laboratory.  Dr. Robert C. Burghardt received his Ph.D. from Wayne State University in 1976 and his postdoctoral training at Harvard Medical School from 1976-78.  He became Professor and Director of the Image Analysis Laboratory. The latter was established in 1987 to serve microscopy and imaging needs of the investigators in the College of Veterinary Medicine, and has since expanded to serve the entire Texas A&M University System. This Lab serves as an Advanced Imaging Facility Core for the interdisciplinary NIEHS-supported Center for Translational Environmental Health Research (CTEHR).

dc_title: Texas Veterinary Medical Diagnostic Laboratory, number 1: Dr. Lelve Gayle
dc_subject: Texas A & M University. College of Veterinary Medicine--Faculty|Diagnostic Services|Gayle, Lelve|Veterinary teachers|Veterinary laboratories|Texas Veterinary Medical Diagnostic Laboratory--Employees
dcterms_isPartOf: College of Veterinary Medicine Image Collection
dcterms_abstract: Dr. Lelve Gayle talks on the phone in his office at the Texas Veterinary Medical Diagnostic Laboratory.  Dr. Gayle earned his doctorate of veterinary medicine degree in 1964 and master of science in veterinary toxicology in 1980, both from Texas A&M University. Prior to joining the Texas Veterinary Medical Diagnostic Laboratory (TVMDL) in 1976, he worked in veterinary private practice and for the U. S. Air Force. Dr. Gayle was president of the Texas Academy of Veterinary Practice in 1985 and president of  the Texas Veterinary Medical Association in 1996. He worked at the TVMDL for 31 years and was executive director from 2002 until July 31, 2007 when he retired. On August 6th, 2017, Dr. Lelve Gayle passed from this life at the age of 77.

dc_subject: Veterinary students|Texas A & M University. dcterms_isPartOf: College of Veterinary Medicine Image Collection
dcterms_abstract: Dean A. A. Price presents the Dallas County Veterinary Medical Association Auxiliary Award to student Robert J. Munger for being the student in the second-year class that demonstrated the most outstanding academic improvement since entering the professional curriculum. Around 1972, Robert J. Munger served as Managing Editor for the "Southwestern Veterinarian" journal, published by the Texas A&M student chapter of the AVMA.  This photograph was taken on July 7, 1972 during the annual award ceremony honoring the academic achievements of undergraduate students at the College of Veterinary Medicine.

dc_subject: Texas Veterinary Medical Association--Membership|Hospitals, Animal|Deans (Education)|Historic buildings|Veterinarians|Agricultural and Mechanical College of Texas. School of Veterinary Medicine--Faculty|Francis, Mark, 1863-1936|Texas Veterinary Medical Association|Congresses and conventions|College buildings|Hospitals, Teaching
dcterms_isPartOf: College of Veterinary Medicine Image Collection
dcterms_abstract: This group of men represent the Texas State Veterinary Association at their 21st annual meeting held in June of 1931. They are standing outside the veterinary hospital that was built in 1908 at the corner of Spence and Robert Street. Dr. Mark Francis is standing in the front row, the fourth person from the right in a light colored suit and dark tie with his hat in his right hand at his side. This was the second building used as a veterinary hospital. It had a central 36X36 foot clinic room, eight box stalls, four tie stalls, an office, feed room, janitors room and hay loft. The east wing of this building later became the anti-hop cholera serum Laboratory until a brick serum lab was built in about 1917. This hospital soon became too small and was enlarged twice by adding more clinic rooms.

dc_title: Launch of Space Chimpanzee Enos's Capsule, the Mercury Atlas 5
dc_subject: Animal space flight|Outer space--Exploration|United States. National Aeronautics and Space Administration|Space Flight|Rockets (Aeronautics)
dcterms_isPartOf: College of Veterinary Medicine Image Collection
dcterms_abstract: The launching of the Mercury Atlas #5 with chimpanzee "Enos" aboard during a test flight.   "Enos" the second chimpanzee to be launched into space on November 29th 1961, and the first to achieve two orbits around the earth. After being brought from the Miami Rare Bird Farm on April 3, 1960 Enos completed more than 1,250 hours of training at the University of Kentucky and the Holloman Air Force Base. During the test flight they had two malfunctions. The capsule overheated and Enos received 76 electrical shocks from a malfunctioning "avoidance conditioning" test. On November 4, 1962 Enos died of shigellosis- related dysentery. His death was unrelated to his earth orbits on Mercury Atlas #5.

dcterms_abstract: This photograph shows the grading and preparation of the site where the new Large Animal Teaching Hospital would later be constructed in 1993.
